name: Trolldom
description: Trolldom, rooted in Scandinavian folklore, represents a complex system of magic deeply intertwined with nature, spirits, and the unseen world. Unlike formal religious practices, Trolldom was a decentralized, localized practice passed down through families or learned from experienced practitioners. It involved harnessing the power of runes, herbs, charms, and incantations to influence events, heal ailments, protect against malevolent forces, and connect with the spirits of the land (landvaettir) and ancestors. The practice often blurred the lines between magic, folk medicine, and religious belief, reflecting a holistic worldview where the spiritual and physical realms were interconnected. Seidr, a related practice often associated with Trolldom, involved trance-like states and divination, sometimes considered a more powerful or specialized form of magic. Although often stigmatized and associated with outcasts or those living on the margins of society, Trolldom played a crucial role in shaping Scandinavian beliefs and traditions, providing a framework for understanding and interacting with the world around them. Its methods involved a deep respect for nature and an understanding of the energies that permeated all things. Christianization gradually suppressed many overt practices, but elements of Trolldom persisted in folklore and folk medicine. Today, there is a growing interest in reviving and reinterpreting Trolldom as a form of spiritual practice and connection to ancestral roots.
subtitle: Scandinavian Folk Magic and Spirit Work

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Mead
keyMyths.description: The Mead of Poetry was created from the blood of Kvasir. It granted wisdom and inspiration to those who drank it, linking it to the power of Seidr and Galdr.

keyMyths.title: Odin's Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Odin hung himself on Yggdrasil to gain knowledge of the runes. This act demonstrates the sacrifice and dedication required to master magical arts.
